s: At the Battle of Chaeronea in 338 BCE, the Sacred Band of Thebes — an elite unit of 300 warriors composed entirely of male couples — fought to the last man against Philip II of Macedon. All 300 died where they stood rather than surrender.
q: What was unique about the Sacred Band of Thebes, who died at Chaeronea in 338 BCE?
a: They were an elite unit composed entirely of male couples
d: A unit of royal guards from the king's household
An elite force of Spartan mercenaries
A regiment of cavalry from neighboring cities
A phalanx of heavily armed hoplites
A force of citizen-soldiers from multiple cities
e: The Sacred Band was based on the idea that lovers would fight more fiercely to protect each other. They had been undefeated for 33 years. Philip's 18-year-old son Alexander (later 'the Great') led the cavalry charge that destroyed them. When Philip saw the 300 bodies lying in formation, he reportedly wept and said 'Perish any man who suspects these men did anything wrong.'.

s: During the Dunkirk evacuation in 1940, over 800 civilian vessels — fishing boats, pleasure yachts, lifeboats, even a paddle steamer — crossed the English Channel to rescue 338,000 Allied soldiers from the beaches, in what Churchill called a 'miracle of deliverance.'
q: Approximately how many Allied soldiers were evacuated from Dunkirk in 1940?
e: The British Expeditionary Force was trapped on the beaches of Dunkirk by the German advance. The Royal Navy couldn't get close enough in large ships, so they commandeered every small vessel available. The 'Little Ships of Dunkirk' made multiple dangerous crossings under constant air attack. Many of these boats still exist and sail in memorial flotillas.

s: The Battle of Hastings on October 14, 1066, saw William the Conqueror defeat King Harold II, ending Anglo-Saxon rule in England.
q: Who won the Battle of Hastings in 1066?
e: William, Duke of Normandy, claimed the English throne and invaded with about 7,000 troops. Harold was killed (possibly by an arrow to the eye), and the Norman Conquest fundamentally changed English language, law, and culture.

s: The Battle of Kadesh in 1274 BCE between Egypt and the Hittites is the earliest battle in recorded history for which we have detailed tactical accounts from BOTH sides — and both claimed victory.
q: What makes the Battle of Kadesh (1274 BCE) unique in military history?
a: It is the earliest battle with detailed accounts from both sides
d: The only battle won by a female pharaoh
The first use of iron weapons in warfare
It involved the first known military alliance
It was fought with elephants and chariots
It marked the end of the Bronze Age
It was the longest single-day battle in history
e: Egyptian Pharaoh Ramesses II covered his temple walls with dramatic carvings of his 'great victory,' while Hittite records also claimed triumph. Modern historians believe it was actually a draw. The battle led to the world's earliest known peace treaty, a copy of which hangs in the UN headquarters.

s: The Battle of Kelja took place in 1939 near Gromovo.

s: The Battle of Lake Trasimene in 217 BCE was the largest ambush in military history. Hannibal hid 50,000 soldiers in the hills around a foggy lakeside, then trapped and destroyed an entire Roman army of 30,000 in just three hours.
q: What natural condition allowed Hannibal to hide 50,000 soldiers at Lake Trasimene?
d: Heavy rain that washed away supply lines
The Roman army being split by a river
Disease that struck the Roman camp
Reinforcements failing to arrive for Rome
e: Roman Consul Flaminius marched his army along a narrow road between Lake Trasimene and the hills without sending scouts ahead. Hannibal had hidden his entire army in the morning fog. When the trap sprung, the Romans were attacked from three sides with the lake at their backs. Most drowned trying to escape. The fighting was so fierce that neither side noticed an earthquake during the battle.

s: The Battle of Lake Vadimo took place in 310 near Lake Vadimo.

s: The Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa in 1212 is considered the most important battle of the Reconquista — a coalition of Spanish Christian kingdoms broke through a chain of enslaved soldiers that the Almohad caliph had literally chained together as a human wall to guard his tent.
q: What grim defensive tactic did the Almohad caliph use at Las Navas de Tolosa in 1212?
a: Chained slave soldiers together as a human wall around his tent
d: Moats filled with water that trapped cavalry
Siege towers that breached the Almohad camp
Mercenary regiments that betrayed the caliph
Enslaved rowers who capsized supply boats
A cavalry feint that broke the main lines
e: The Almohad caliph Muhammad al-Nasir surrounded his command tent with thousands of slave soldiers chained to stakes, creating a living barricade. When the Christian knights broke through this grim defense, the Almohad army collapsed. The battle shattered Muslim military power in Iberia and opened the way for the eventual completion of the Reconquista in 1492.

s: The Battle of Le Mans took place in 1871 near Le Mans.
q: Near which French city did a Franco-Prussian War battle occur in 1871?
e: In 1871, the clash at Le Mans changed the course of history!

s: At the Battle of the Little Bighorn in 1876, Custer's 7th Cavalry was annihilated in under an hour by a combined force of Lakota, Northern Cheyenne, and Arapaho warriors — but the Native Americans' victory ultimately accelerated the US military campaign against them.
q: Approximately how long did it take for Custer's detachment to be wiped out at Little Bighorn?
d: Approximately one full day of conflict
Over two days of continuous battle
Nearly half a day of fighting
e: Custer divided his 700-man regiment and attacked a village of 7,000-8,000 people. His 210-man detachment was surrounded and wiped out so quickly that no one in his command survived to tell the story. The defeat shocked America and led to a massive military response that crushed Native resistance within a year.

s: The Battle of Ölper took place in 1761 near Ölper.

s: The Battle of Manzikert in 1071 was a disaster caused by betrayal: when Byzantine Emperor Romanos IV was surrounded by Seljuk Turks, his own general Andronikos Doukas deliberately withdrew his forces and left the emperor to be captured — the first Byzantine emperor captured in battle in over 600 years.
q: Why did the Byzantine Emperor lose the Battle of Manzikert in 1071?
a: His own general deliberately withdrew forces and betrayed him
d: The Emperor was wounded and had to retreat
The Byzantine army was simply outnumbered
A sudden sandstorm disoriented Byzantine forces
The Emperor made a tactical miscalculation
e: Doukas wanted the throne for his family and saw an opportunity. After capturing Romanos, the Seljuk Sultan Alp Arslan treated him with remarkable generosity — then released him. But when Romanos returned, his own court had already replaced him and he was blinded and exiled. Manzikert opened Anatolia to Turkish settlement, eventually leading to the Ottoman Empire.

s: During the First Battle of the Marne in 1914, Paris was saved when the French military requisitioned 600 taxicabs to rush 6,000 reserve soldiers to the front lines — the first time motor vehicles were used to transport troops in battle.
q: How were 6,000 French reserve soldiers transported to the First Battle of the Marne in 1914?
d: By military trains with passenger cars
On foot in a forced march
By canal boats down the Seine
e: With the German army just 30 miles from Paris, French General Gallieni commandeered every taxi in the city. Each cab made multiple round trips ferrying soldiers to the front. While the 'Taxis of the Marne' carried only a fraction of the troops needed, they became a powerful symbol of French determination and marked the dawn of motorized warfare.

s: The Battle of Midway (June 1942) was the turning point of the Pacific War, where the US Navy sank four Japanese aircraft carriers.
q: How many Japanese aircraft carriers were sunk at the Battle of Midway?
e: American codebreakers had decrypted Japanese plans, enabling an ambush. The loss of four fleet carriers — Akagi, Kaga, Soryu, and Hiryu — along with their experienced pilots was a blow from which Japan's navy never recovered.

s: The Battle of Nicopolis in 1396 was the last major crusade, and it failed spectacularly because French knights insisted on charging first for glory — they rode straight into sharpened stakes and were slaughtered, dooming the entire Christian army.
q: Why did the last major crusade fail at Nicopolis in 1396?
a: French knights insisted on charging first and rode into sharpened stakes
d: Ottoman archers ambushed them from hidden positions
They charged into a swamp and got stuck
A sudden rainstorm flooded the battlefield
Their supply lines were cut by the Ottomans
They were trapped by a feigned retreat
e: An international coalition of European knights gathered to stop Ottoman expansion. The French demanded the honor of leading the attack despite warnings about hidden Turkish defenses. They charged uphill into rows of planted stakes that impaled their horses, then were cut down by janissaries. The disaster ensured Ottoman dominance in the Balkans for centuries.

s: The Battle of Okinawa in 1945 was so devastating that both the American and Japanese commanding generals died during it — making it the only major WWII battle where both sides lost their top commander.
q: What was unusual about the Battle of Okinawa regarding its commanding generals?
a: Both the American and Japanese commanders died during the battle
d: The American general died from suicide bombing
Neither commander died, but both were wounded
The American general was captured and executed
Both committed ritual suicide after defeat
The American general was killed in a friendly fire incident
e: Japanese General Ushijima committed ritual suicide (seppuku) as defeat became inevitable. American General Simon Buckner was killed by enemy artillery just days before the battle ended, making him the highest-ranking US officer killed by enemy fire in WWII. The battle's extreme casualties influenced the decision to use atomic bombs instead of invading mainland Japan.

s: At the Battle of Pharsalus in 48 BCE, Julius Caesar defeated Pompey's army that outnumbered him 2-to-1 by ordering his soldiers to stab the enemy cavalry in the face — knowing that the young aristocrats on horseback would flinch and flee rather than risk facial scars.
q: What unusual tactic did Caesar order his soldiers to use against Pompey's cavalry at Pharsalus?
a: Stab them in the face, knowing they would flee to avoid scars
d: Target their horses' legs to immobilize them
Use fire to frighten their mounts
Form testudo formations to resist cavalry charges
Concentrate arrows on their shields to break formation
Throw nets to entangle their weapons
e: Caesar noticed that Pompey's cavalry were wealthy young Romans who valued their appearance. He instructed his infantry to thrust their javelins at the riders' faces instead of their bodies. The tactic worked perfectly — the cavalry broke and fled, exposing Pompey's flank. This victory made Caesar the undisputed master of Rome.

s: The Battle of Plassey in 1757, which gave Britain control of India, was largely won through bribery rather than fighting. The British had secretly paid off Mir Jafar, the commander of most of the Bengali army, to simply not fight — and most of his troops stood idle while the battle raged.
q: How did the British effectively win the Battle of Plassey in 1757?
a: By bribing the enemy's main general to not fight
d: Through a surprise night attack using river gunboats
By releasing trained elephants into the enemy lines
By exploiting a natural flood that divided the army
By negotiating a secret ceasefire at dawn
By recruiting mercenary generals from other kingdoms
e: Robert Clive's force of 3,000 faced Siraj ud-Daulah's army of 50,000. But Clive had spent weeks bribing Mir Jafar with promises of the throne. During the battle, two-thirds of the Bengali army never engaged. Mir Jafar became puppet ruler, and the East India Company gained control of Bengal's enormous tax revenues — funding British expansion across the entire subcontinent.
